scenario_results.reason: The lane produced a restaurant-specific handoff package with explicit ownership, KDS and ticket artifacts, and blocker state.
Missing BEO, allergen, pickup, and destination-acceptance prerequisites were treated as blockers rather than a routable handoff.
Duplicate ownership and conflicting fire or pickup schedules were escalated with an owner map instead of dropped into generic kitchen coordination.
Reservation, payment, and modifier conflicts were preserved as an evidence-backed mismatch and routed without unsupported refund adjudication.
Refund, fee, and comp requests outside documented authority were rejected and handed off with the right records.
Guest-safety and suspicious-payment conditions were escalated to the correct owners rather than over-resolved in-lane.
The lane refused to absorb cooking, POS update, and guest-resolution execution work and returned boundary-safe routing guidance.
High-volume rush handling preserved queue control and did not bypass prerequisite or policy checks.
Missing tenant authority and station-owner maps correctly triggered a low-confidence blocked-dependency escalation.
Conflicting public and tenant guest-impact handling rules were escalated for human resolution instead of synthesized into a definitive instruction.
Stricter tenant banquet and hotel controls were applied without breaking the public baseline or inventing new lane authority.
Closure was only confirmed after destination-owner proof and required ticket, timing, and guest-impact artifacts were explicit.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the standard 0.90 threshold while keeping the lane specific to kitchen production coordination, ticket and prep dependencies, owner routing, and customer-impact boundaries.

Functionality mapping and scenario coverage include all shared abilities, all coordination-handoffs-and-routing abilities, and all retail-consumer-and-hospitality abilities required by the issue contract.
